activities_projects.title: Chemical Nature of Pure Substances
activities_projects.objective: To understand that scientific purity is based on the chemical nature of constituent particles[cite: 939].
steps: Analyze a sample of milk and research its constituents (water, fat, proteins)[cite: 939].
Compare this with a sample of sugar, which contains only one kind of pure matter[cite: 948].
List the characteristic properties of a pure substance regardless of its source[cite: 949].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Concludes that a pure substance consists of a single type of particle[cite: 940].
activities_projects.evaluation: Explain why milk is scientifically a mixture even if labeled 'pure' (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Variable Composition in Homogeneous Mixtures
activities_projects.objective: To demonstrate that homogeneous mixtures can have different intensities and compositions[cite: 965].
steps: Add one spatula of copper sulphate to Beaker A and two spatulas to Beaker B[cite: 956, 957].
Stir both and compare the uniformity and intensity of the color[cite: 959, 964].
Discuss if the composition is uniform throughout each beaker[cite: 960].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands that solutions have uniform composition but can vary in concentration[cite: 960, 965].
activities_projects.evaluation: Differentiate between a dilute and a concentrated solution based on this activity (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Properties of a True Solution
activities_projects.objective: To identify characteristics of solutions that make them unique from other mixtures[cite: 1022].
steps: Dissolve salt in water and check if particles are visible to the naked eye[cite: 1024].
Pass a beam of light through the solution and check for the Tyndall effect[cite: 1025].
Try to separate the salt using filtration and check for stability after leaving it undisturbed[cite: 1027, 1028].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Recognizes that true solutions have particles smaller than 1 nm and are highly stable[cite: 1024, 1028].
activities_projects.evaluation: List four properties of a solution based on your observations (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Calculating Solution Concentration
activities_projects.objective: To apply mathematical methods to express the concentration of a solution[cite: 1049].
steps: Measure the mass of the solute and the solvent[cite: 1068, 1069].
Calculate the total mass of the solution[cite: 1071].
Use the mass-by-mass percentage formula to find the concentration[cite: 1050, 1073].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Learns to quantify the amount of solute in a given mass of solution[cite: 1048].
activities_projects.evaluation: Calculate the mass percentage if the solute mass is doubled (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Investigating Suspensions
activities_projects.objective: To observe the behavior and properties of heterogeneous suspensions[cite: 1091].
steps: Mix chalk powder in water and observe the visibility of particles[cite: 1095].
Test for light scattering while the particles are suspended[cite: 1077].
Leave the mixture undisturbed and check for stability and light scattering after settling[cite: 1078, 1080].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Identifies that suspensions are unstable and can be separated by filtration[cite: 1078, 1079].
activities_projects.evaluation: Explain why a suspension stops scattering light after the particles settle (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: The Tyndall Effect in Colloids
activities_projects.objective: To analyze how colloidal particles interact with light compared to solutions[cite: 1087].
steps: Prepare a mixture of milk and water[cite: 1081].
Direct a beam of light through the mixture and observe the path visibility[cite: 1086].
Compare this to a copper sulphate solution which does not show the effect[cite: 1106].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands that colloidal particles are large enough to scatter light but too small to be seen individually[cite: 1085, 1114].
activities_projects.evaluation: Provide a real-life example of the Tyndall effect observed in nature [cite: 1110] (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Classifying Colloids by State
activities_projects.objective: To identify the dispersed phase and dispersing medium in common colloids[cite: 1120].
steps: Examine common items like fog and shaving cream[cite: 1129].
Identify the state of the dispersed particles and the dispersing medium for each[cite: 1121].
Classify them into types like Aerosol, Foam, Emulsion, or Gel[cite: 1129].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Differentiates between various types of colloids found in daily life[cite: 1123].
activities_projects.evaluation: Identify the dispersed phase and dispersing medium in 'Milk of Magnesia' (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Physical and Chemical Change Analysis
activities_projects.objective: To distinguish between changes in physical properties and chemical composition[cite: 1137, 1142].
steps: Compare the physical properties of water and oil (odour, inflammability)[cite: 1139].
Observe the burning of a candle and identify both physical and chemical changes occurring[cite: 1145].
Explain how chemical changes result in the formation of new substances[cite: 1143].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Recognizes that burning is a chemical change while state interconversion is physical[cite: 1137, 1141].
activities_projects.evaluation: Classify the boiling of water to form steam as a physical or chemical change and justify (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Elementary Classification: Metals vs. Non-metals
activities_projects.objective: To define an element and compare properties of its main categories[cite: 1157, 1158].
steps: Test the samples for lustre, malleability, and ductility[cite: 1160, 1162, 1163].
Check the heat and electrical conductivity of each sample[cite: 1161, 1167].
Research elements like mercury and bromine that are liquid at room temperature[cite: 1165, 1178].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Differentiates between metals, non-metals, and metalloids based on experimental properties[cite: 1158, 1173].
activities_projects.evaluation: Name two elements that act as metalloids and explain why [cite: 1174] (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Formation of a Chemical Compound
activities_projects.objective: To distinguish a compound from a mixture based on properties and separation methods[cite: 1218].
steps: Group 1: Mix iron and sulphur. Test with a magnet and add acid[cite: 1195, 1196, 1202, 1206].
Group 2: Heat the mixture strongly until red hot, then repeat the magnet and acid tests[cite: 1198, 1199, 1202, 1206].
Compare the properties of the gas obtained (Hydrogen vs. Hydrogen Sulphide)[cite: 1208, 1209].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands that a compound has totally different properties from its constituent elements[cite: 1228, 1232].
activities_projects.evaluation: Summarize three differences between a mixture and a compound based on this experiment [cite: 1218] (10 marks).
